blurb: Independent UK research institute on data & AI for people and society (part of the Nuffield Foundation). Strong named-author publication output across AI law/policy, public-sector AI, and public participation.

hiring.note: Official working-at-Ada page resolved; no machine-readable ATS found. Use as careers source and manually check role pages.

publications.title: 'I love it, but I hate it': young people's experiences and expectations of growing up digital
publications.summary: Report on young people's lived experience of a digital childhood, feeding Ada's public-participation and AI-governance work.

publications.title: Measuring up: evaluating claims about AI and productivity in the UK public sector
publications.summary: Policy briefing interrogating productivity claims made for AI in UK public services — an AI-evaluation lens on government adoption hype.

publications.title: Navigating the future: a landscape review of AI in career guidance for young people
publications.summary: Landscape review of AI tools in career guidance, examining public-sector deployment and equity implications.

publications.title: Scribe and prejudice? Exploring the use of AI transcription tools in social care
publications.summary: Report on AI transcription tools in social care — bias and evaluation risks in a sensitive public-sector setting.

news.title: Ada Lovelace Institute response to social media ban for under-16s
news.summary: Ada responds to the UK government's announced ban on social media for under-16s and restrictions on harmful features — part of the June 2026 cluster of cross-sector online-safety policy responses.

news.title: Young people urge government to establish age-specific protections for online spaces
news.summary: New peer-research (part of the Nuffield Foundation's 'Grown up?' programme) in which young people call for age-specific online protections to safeguard future generations.

news.title: Policymakers must apply higher standards of evidence to claims about AI and public sector productivity
news.summary: Ada argues policymakers should demand higher evidentiary standards before accepting claims that AI boosts public-sector productivity — a characteristically evidence-first intervention into the gov-AI-efficiency narrative.

notes: [publications backfill 2026-06-26] Harvested from the org's own publications page. Some items lack a visible date on the index — null rather than fabricated; refine on a later pass. [date-refine 2026-07-02] Attempted first-party dating of the 'I love it, but I hate it' report: the Ada Lovelace report URL now 301-redirects to the co-publisher Nuffield Foundation (nuffieldfoundation.org/.../grown-up/i-love-it-but-i-hate-it, HTTP 200). That page carries NO JSON-LD datePublished and no visible publish-date element — only article:modified_time 2026-05-21. Held null (honest no-op): using the modified-date would repeat the earlier Ada Lovelace article:modified_time trap (modified/republish ≠ publish). The 'Month YYYY' strings in the body are footnote 'accessed' dates, not the report date. Revisit if a first-party publish date surfaces (e.g. report PDF frontmatter).
